one_line_takeaway: A 46-study systematic mapping of label-efficient code vulnerability detection finds token-based binary-classification pipelines at function-level granularity dominate the field, that roughly a quarter of studies report no explicit label budget (mostly LLM-based, zero-/few-shot), and that label savings are typically not free but shift cost into preprocessing, training complexity, or LLM inference.
key_finding: Representation: in the 46-study Main pool, token-based representations dominate (29 studies), while graph-based and knowledge-based representations appear in 7 studies each and explicit hybrid (token+graph) designs are rare (3 studies); LLM-based pipelines are overwhelmingly token-centric, while structure/external-signal use concentrates in semi-/self-supervised and contrastive settings. Task framing: binary vulnerable/non-vulnerable classification dominates (36 studies) over CWE-typed multi-class classification (10 studies), the latter concentrated mainly in few-shot/meta-learning work. Granularity: function-level-only evaluation dominates (28/46 studies per Table 2), with finer-grained (line/statement) or multi-granular settings comparatively rare. Datasets/languages: evaluation concentrates on a small set of recurring benchmarks (Big-Vul most frequent, followed by project-specific corpora such as FFmpeg/QEMU and curated sets like ReVeal/Devign) and is heavily skewed toward C/C++, with Java and Python sporadic and PHP/Ruby/Go/Solidity rarely represented. Label-budget reporting (RQ3, Main Set n=46): 8/46 studies (17.4%) report K-shot budgets, 6/46 (13.0%) report fraction-based (percentage-labeled) budgets, and 11/46 (23.9%) fall into a zero-shot/no-training regime with implicit or qualitative supervision, of which 9 are LLM-based approaches; a substantial remainder leaves budgets unspecified/unclear. Compute cost (RQ4, Main Set n=46): 28/46 studies report some compute-cost signal (time, epochs, API calls), 34/46 report compute resources/hardware, only 22/46 report both, and 6/46 report neither; API/hosted-LLM usage is mentioned in 14/46 studies but explicit monetary cost analysis is rare. The authors observe a 'cost-shifting' pattern: label savings in graph/hybrid pipelines shift cost to preprocessing/graph construction, in semi-/self-/contrastive pipelines to training complexity, and in LLM pipelines to inference-time cost (context length, retrieval, multiple passes, API constraints) -- i.e., label-efficient CVD is not uniformly cheap.
core_contribution: This is a systematic mapping study (SoK-style survey), not an empirical method paper. It synthesizes label-efficient code vulnerability detection (CVD) research around five paradigm families -- semi-/self-supervised learning, contrastive learning, few-shot/meta-learning, prompt tuning, and LLM-based prompting/parameter-efficient adaptation -- and connects them to code representation choices (token, graph, hybrid, knowledge-based). The authors' own stated contributions are: (1) the first structured mapping of label-efficient CVD methods organized by paradigm family and mechanism; (2) synthesis of how representation and evaluation design shape feasibility, linking representation families to dataset and granularity choices; (3) consolidation of evidence on label-budget reporting, generalization, and compute/operational cost into a Design Map (Table 3) and a constraint-first Practitioner Decision Guide (Table 4); (4) a public repository with curated paper list, extracted metadata, and figure-generation scripts. The corpus was built via a systematic search of ACM DL, Scopus, ScienceDirect, and Google Scholar (searches last run October 2025, coverage window Jan 2006-Sep 2025): 9,983 initial records -> 1,994 after a precision-oriented 'vulnerab*' title/abstract filter -> 121 after title/abstract screening -> 69 after full-text screening -> +47 via snowballing -> a final Main Set of 46 CVD-specific studies (used for core synthesis) plus a disjoint Inspiration Set of 64 adjacent-SE studies (used only to contextualize mechanisms, never aggregated with Main-Set results).
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is a literature survey/mapping study, not an attack, defense, or detection-system paper. It does not propose or evaluate a detector against an adversary and defines no threat model of its own; it catalogues the supervision assumptions (label scarcity, noisy/uneven labeling across projects, languages and CWE types) that motivate the surveyed label-efficient CVD literature.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations/threats to validity (Section 9, and RQ5's synthesis of author-stated limitations across the corpus) include: (1) heterogeneous, non-standardized label-budget reporting across studies (K-shot vs. percentage vs. absolute count vs. implicit supervision) that limits direct comparability and forced the authors to avoid curve-based meta-analysis; (2) reporting omissions across the surveyed corpus -- missing artifacts (code, prompts, preprocessing scripts) and incomplete disclosure of splits, deduplication, compute, and inference settings -- that hinder verification and replication of the underlying studies; (3) narrow dataset/language coverage in the surveyed literature itself (concentration on a handful of benchmarks, heavy C/C++ skew) that limits what conclusions about label-efficiency generalization the survey can support; (4) evaluation metric concentration on F1/precision/recall in the underlying literature, with limited use of imbalance-robust (MCC/PR-AUC) or effort-aware (top-k inspection) metrics, which the authors flag as a comparability risk rather than a finding about any single method; (5) function-level-granularity dominance in the surveyed studies, limiting evidence about localization-oriented (line/statement) label efficiency; (6) the Main-vs-Inspiration corpus split is a deliberate construct-validity control (Inspiration Set never aggregated into CVD-specific performance claims), but this also means many 'transferable mechanism' claims in RQ1 rest on adjacent-task (non-CVD) evidence rather than CVD-validated results.

datasets_used: Big-Vul (most frequently used benchmark in the surveyed Main pool)
DiverseVul (referenced as a curated vulnerability dataset in background discussion)
SARD/Juliet (referenced as a synthetic benchmark corpus in background discussion)
quantitative_results: Study selection funnel: 9,983 initial records -> 1,994 after dedup/vulnerab* filter -> 121 after title/abstract screening -> 69 after full-text screening -> 46 Main-set + 64 Inspiration-set studies after snowballing (47 added via snowballing)
Representation prevalence in Main pool (n=46): token-based 29 studies, graph-based 7 studies, knowledge-based 7 studies, hybrid (token+graph) 3 studies
Task type in Main pool: binary detection 36 studies, CWE typing 10 studies
Granularity (Table 2): function-level-only 28 studies; function+statement 3; function+line 3; project-level-only 3; statement-only 2; line-only 2; other combinations 1-2 studies each
Label-budget reporting (Main Set n=46): K-shot 8/46 (17.4%); fraction-based (percentage-labeled) 6/46 (13.0%); zero-shot/no-training with implicit supervision 11/46 (23.9%), of which 9 are LLM-based

Compute-cost reporting (Main Set n=46): compute-cost signal reported by 28/46; compute resources/hardware reported by 34/46; both reported by 22/46; neither reported by 6/46; API/hosted-LLM usage mentioned in 14/46 studies
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the conventional sense -- this survey does not run its own experiments or compare baselines head-to-head. It positions itself against 11 prior CVD/ML-security surveys (Table 1: [33], [60], [86], [90], [4], [36], [71], [114], [32], [83], [27]), noting that none combine explicit CVD scope, LLM coverage, a label-efficiency organizing axis, and a label-budget reporting lens simultaneously, whereas this survey claims to cover all four dimensions.
